| Guar Gum | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Category | Thickeners and stabilizers |

Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients

**Guar gum** is a thickener derived from seeds of a legume native to South Asia.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:4-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The gum is sold as a beige powder. It can be hydrated in cool water,[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3) whereupon it will bind up to 100-fold its weight of water and impart high viscosity to the mixture.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) However, it cannot actually make gels without other additives.[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3) Notably, this viscous mixture is not particularly stable in highly acidic mixtures (< pH 4).[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) The gum can sometimes result in an undesirable starchy texture.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Make sure to purchase food-grade guar gum, since non-food grades exist. Keep guar gum in an airtight container away from light, heat, and moisture.

## Use

[edit | edit source]

The water-binding capacity of guar gum makes it particularly useful in gluten-free baking and frozen desserts. In the former, it adds structure and functions as a binder;[[6]](#cite_note-6) in the latter, it reduces the development of ice crystals.[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3) It also adds body and mouthfeel to products where a creamy texture is desired.[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

In some cases, xanthan gum may act as a substitute for guar gum.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## Recipes

[edit | edit source]
